front: Composer: worked as kapellmeister to the Elector of Hanover before settling in England; born in the same year as J.S. Bach and Domenico Scarlatti
Composer: went blind in final years; John Christopher Smith served as his amanuensis
Composer: rival was Giovanni Bononcini; the two were compared in John Byram's epigram that coined 'Tweedledum and Tweedledee'
Composer: abandoned Italian opera after the success of an oratorio containing the line 'And he shall reign for ever and ever'
Composer: Opus 4 organ concertos are the earliest concertos for chamber organ; performed as interludes during oratorio performances at Covent Garden
Oratorio: libretto by Charles Jennens; audiences stand during Part II's closing chorus by tradition begun at a London performance attended by King George II
Oratorio: opening 'Sinfony' is an E minor French overture; includes a 'Pifa' (Pastoral Symphony) in 12/8 inspired by shepherd-bagpipers
Oratorio: Pifa opens with first violins playing ascending C, D, E, F, G, A over a bass drone on C
Oratorio: Part I contains 'Comfort ye my people' and 'Ev'ry valley shall be exalted'; Hallelujah chorus melody is long D, low A, B, A
Oratorio: re-orchestrated by Mozart in 1789 with substantial wind additions; musicologist Rudolf Steglich identified the ascending fourth as its unifying motif
Suite: commissioned for King George I's barge trip down the River Thames; D-major suite contains an Alla Hornpipe movement
Suite: three suites — F major (French horn prominent; possibly the first English music to feature it), D major (trumpet-heavy), G major (flute-heavy)
Suite: commemorates Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle; premiere structure by Giovanni Servandoni caught fire; originally for wind ensemble with 24 oboes and 12 bassoons
Suite: movements 'La Paix' (Largo alla siciliana) and 'La Rejouissance'; called for 9 natural trumpets and 9 natural horns
Anthem: opens with rising string arpeggios before a 7-part chorus enters; performed at every British coronation since 1727; sets text from the first Book of Kings
Opera: Julius Caesar's aria 'Va tacito e nascosto' features a natural horn obbligato and compares him to a hunter; premiered by castrato Senesino
Opera: Cleopatra seduces Caesar in 'V'adoro pupille' with a second orchestra playing offstage; Sesto swears revenge in 'Svegliatevi nel core'
Opera: Saracen king Argante falls in love with captive Almirena, who sings 'Lascia ch'io pianga'; may contain the first piccolo part ever written
Opera: opens with Xerxes addressing the shade of a plane tree in 'Ombra mai fu'; marked larghetto but known as 'Handel's Largo'
Oratorio: depicts Plagues of Egypt; dotted rhythms represent frogs leaping and demisemiquavers represent flies buzzing in 'Their land brought forth frogs'
Oratorio: bass rage aria breaks off mid-section as a two-octave descending scale depicts spear-hurling; interlude reused in 'The Cuckoo and the Nightingale' organ concerto
Oratorio: Act 3 opens with the 'Arrival of the Queen of Sheba' sinfonia for oboes and strings; 'Nightingale Chorus' features two obbligato flutes imitating birds
Oratorio: Simon sings 'Arm, arm ye brave'; chorus sings 'See, the conqu'ring hero comes'; lyrics by Thomas Morell proclaim 'Sound the trumpets! Beat the drums!'
Suite: Air and Variations finale of Harpsichord Suite No. 5 in E major; theme begins E, G-sharp, F-sharp, high B; repeated B pedal tone likened to a blacksmith's hammer
Suite: G minor Passacaglia and D minor Sarabande from two keyboard suites arranged for violin and viola by Johan Halvorsen
back: The Harmonious Blacksmith / Handel keyboard suites

front: Concerto: set of 12 concerti grossi modeled on Corelli's Op. 6; No. 6 in G minor has an E-flat major musette as its central movement; one arranged by Schoenberg as Concerto for String Quartet
